paragraphs.context: This Act may be called the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint, and different dates may be appointed for different provisions of this Sanhita. Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Sanhita and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within India. Any person liable, by any law for the time being in force in India, to be tried for an offence committed beyond India shall be dealt with according to the provisions of this Sanhita for any act committed beyond India in the same manner as if such act had been committed within India.

qas.question: What is the title of this Act?

answers.text: Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.

qas.question: When does the Act come into force?

answers.text: On such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint.

qas.question: Can different dates be appointed for different provisions of the Sanhita?

answers.text: Yes, different dates may be appointed for different provisions of this Sanhita.

qas.question: Who is liable to punishment under the Sanhita?

answers.text: Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Sanhita and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within India.

qas.question: How are offences committed beyond India dealt with under this Sanhita?

answers.text: According to the provisions of this Sanhita for any act committed beyond India in the same manner as if such act had been committed within India.

paragraphs.context: The provisions of this Sanhita shall also apply to any offence committed by any citizen of India in any place without and beyond India; any person on any ship or aircraft registered in India wherever it may be; any person in any place without and beyond India committing offence targeting a computer resource located in India.

answers.text: Any citizen of India in any place without and beyond India.

qas.question: Does the Sanhita apply to offences committed on Indian ships or aircraft?

answers.text: Yes, it applies to any person on any ship or aircraft registered in India wherever it may be.

qas.question: What offences targeting computer resources are covered under the Sanhita?

answers.text: Any person in any place without and beyond India committing offence targeting a computer resource located in India.

paragraphs.context: CHAPTER II OF PUNISHMENTS: 4. The punishments to which offenders are liable under the provisions of this Sanhita are— (a) Death; (b) Imprisonment for life; (c) Imprisonment, which is of two descriptions, namely: (1) Rigorous, that is, with hard labour; (2) Simple; (d) Forfeiture of property; (e) Fine; (f) Community Service.

qas.question: What are the types of punishments provided under this Sanhita?

answers.text: Death, Imprisonment for life, Rigorous imprisonment, Simple imprisonment, Forfeiture of property, Fine, and Community Service.

paragraphs.context: Commutation of sentence: 5. The appropriate Government may, without the consent of the offender, commute any punishment under this Sanhita to any other punishment in accordance with section 474 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023. Explanation: For the purposes of this section the expression 'appropriate Government' means,— (a) in cases where the sentence is a sentence of death or is for an offence against any law relating to a matter to which the executive power of the Union extends, the Central Government; and (b) in cases where the sentence (whether of death or not) is for an offence against any law relating to a matter to which the executive power of the State extends, the Government of the State within which the offender is sentenced.

qas.question: Who has the authority to commute a sentence under this Sanhita?

answers.text: The appropriate Government, which can be either the Central Government or the State Government depending on the case.

qas.question: What does the 'appropriate Government' refer to in the context of commutation of sentences?

answers.text: In cases where the sentence is of death or related to a matter under the Union’s executive power, it is the Central Government; in cases related to the State's executive power, it is the Government of the State.

paragraphs.context: Fractions of terms of punishment: 6. In calculating fractions of terms of punishment, imprisonment for life shall be reckoned as equivalent to imprisonment for twenty years unless otherwise provided.

qas.question: How is imprisonment for life calculated in terms of punishment fractions?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life is reckoned as equivalent to imprisonment for twenty years unless otherwise provided.

paragraphs.context: 7. qas.question: What discretion does the Court have in cases of imprisonment of either description?

answers.text: The Court may direct that imprisonment shall be wholly rigorous, wholly simple, or partly rigorous and partly simple.

qas.question: When is harm caused to a person under twelve years of age or a person of unsound mind not an offence?

answers.text: If it is done in good faith for their benefit, by or with the consent of their guardian or lawful charge.

paragraphs.context: A, in good faith, for his child’s benefit without his child’s consent, has his child cut for the stone by a surgeon knowing it to be likely that the operation will cause the child’s death, but not intending to cause the child’s death. A is within the exception, in as much as his object was the cure of the child.

qas.question: Why is A not guilty of an offence for subjecting his child to a life-threatening surgery?

answers.text: Because A acted in good faith for his child’s benefit and with the object of curing the child.

paragraphs.context: Where anything which is an offence is made up of parts, any of which parts is itself an offence, the offender shall not be punished with the punishment of more than one of such his offences, unless it be so expressly provided. Where anything is an offence falling within two or more separate definitions of any law in force for the time being by which offences are defined or punished, or several acts, of which one or more than one would by itself or themselves constitute an offence, constitute, when combined, a different offence, the offender shall not be punished with a more severe punishment than the Court which tries him could award for any one of such offences.

qas.question: What happens when an offence is made up of parts, each of which is itself an offence?

answers.text: The offender shall not be punished with the punishment of more than one of such his offences, unless it be so expressly provided.

qas.question: What is the punishment when an offence falls under multiple definitions of law?

answers.text: The offender shall not be punished with a more severe punishment than the Court which tries him could award for any one of such offences.

qas.question: What happens if several acts constitute a different offence when combined?

answers.text: The offender shall not be punished with a more severe punishment than the Court which tries him could award for any one of such offences.

paragraphs.context: A gives Z fifty strokes with a stick. Here A may have committed the offence of voluntarily causing hurt to Z by the whole beating, and also by each of the blows which make up the whole beating. If A were liable to punishment for every blow, he might be imprisoned for fifty years, one for each blow. But he is liable only to one punishment for the whole beating. But, if, while A is beating Z, Y interferes, and A intentionally strikes Y, here, as the blow given to Y is no part of the act whereby A voluntarily causes hurt to Z, A is liable to one punishment for voluntarily causing hurt to Z, and to another for the blow given to Y.

qas.question: How many punishments is A liable for if he gives Z fifty strokes with a stick?

answers.text: He is liable only to one punishment for the whole beating.

qas.question: What happens if A intentionally strikes Y while beating Z?

answers.text: A is liable to one punishment for voluntarily causing hurt to Z, and to another for the blow given to Y.

paragraphs.context: In all cases in which judgment is given that a person is guilty of one of several offences specified in the judgment, but that it is doubtful of which of these offences he is guilty, the offender shall be punished for the offence for which the lowest punishment is provided if the same punishment is not provided for all.

qas.question: How is an offender punished if it is doubtful which offence he is guilty of?

answers.text: The offender shall be punished for the offence for which the lowest punishment is provided if the same punishment is not provided for all.

paragraphs.context: Whenever any person is convicted of an offence for which under this Sanhita the Court has power to sentence him to rigorous imprisonment, the Court may, by its sentence, order that the offender shall be kept in solitary confinement for any portion or portions of the imprisonment to which he is sentenced, not exceeding three months in the whole.

qas.question: What is the maximum duration of solitary confinement for an offender?

answers.text: Not exceeding three months in the whole.

paragraphs.context: A time not exceeding one month if the term of imprisonment shall not exceed six months; a time not exceeding two months if the term of imprisonment shall exceed six months and shall not exceed one year; a time not exceeding three months if the term of imprisonment shall exceed one year.

qas.question: What is the maximum duration of solitary confinement if the imprisonment term does not exceed six months?

answers.text: A time not exceeding one month.

qas.question: What is the maximum duration of solitary confinement if the imprisonment term is between six months and one year?

answers.text: A time not exceeding two months.

qas.question: What is the maximum duration of solitary confinement if the imprisonment term exceeds one year?

answers.text: A time not exceeding three months.

paragraphs.context: Whoever, having been convicted by a Court in India, of an offence punishable under Chapter X or Chapter XVII of this Sanhita with imprisonment of either description for a term of three years or upwards, shall be guilty of any offence punishable under either of those Chapters with like imprisonment for the like term, shall be subject for every such subsequent offence to imprisonment for life, or to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years.

qas.question: What is the enhanced punishment for subsequent offences under Chapter X or Chapter XVII?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life, or to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years.

paragraphs.context: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.

qas.question: When is an act not considered an offence due to a mistake of fact?

answers.text: When a person in good faith believes himself to be bound by law to do it.

paragraphs.context: A, a soldier, fires on a mob by the order of his superior officer, in conformity with the commands of the law. A has committed no offence.

qas.question: Why has A committed no offence when firing on a mob?

answers.text: Because A acted in conformity with the commands of the law and by order of his superior officer.

paragraphs.context: A, an officer of a Court, being ordered by that Court to arrest Y, and, after due enquiry, believing Z to be Y, arrests Z. A has committed no offence.

qas.question: Why has A committed no offence when mistakenly arresting Z?

answers.text: Because A, after due enquiry, believed Z to be Y.

paragraphs.context: Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of any power which is, or which in good faith he believes to be, given to him by law.

qas.question: When is an act by a Judge not considered an offence?

answers.text: When acting judicially in the exercise of any power which he believes in good faith to be given to him by law.

paragraphs.context: Where no sum is expressed to which a fine may extend, the amount of fine to which the offender is liable is unlimited, but shall not be excessive. In every case of an offence–– (a) punishable with imprisonment as well as fine, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, whether with or without imprisonment; (b) punishable with imprisonment or fine, or with fine only, in which the offender is sentenced to a fine, it shall be competent to the Court which sentences such offender to direct by the sentence that, in default of payment of the fine, the offender shall suffer imprisonment for a certain term, in which imprisonment shall be in excess of any other imprisonment to which he may have been sentenced or to which he may be liable under a commutation of a sentence. The term for which the Court directs the offender to be imprisoned in default of payment of a fine shall not exceed one-fourth of the term of imprisonment which is the maximum fixed for the offence, if the offence be punishable with imprisonment as well as fine. The imprisonment which the Court imposes in default of payment of a fine or in default of community service may be of any description to which the offender might have been sentenced for the offence. If the offence is punishable with fine or community service, the imprisonment which the Court imposes in default of payment of the fine or in default of community service shall be simple, and the term for which the Court directs the offender to be imprisoned, in default of payment of fine or in default of community service, shall not exceed,— (a) two months when the amount of the fine does not exceed five thousand rupees; (b) four months when the amount of the fine does not exceed ten thousand rupees; and (c) one year in any other case. The imprisonment which is imposed in default of payment of a fine shall terminate whenever that fine is either paid or levied by process of law. If, before the expiration of the term of imprisonment fixed in default of payment, such a proportion of the fine be paid or levied that the term of imprisonment suffered in default of payment is not less than proportional to the part of the fine still unpaid, the imprisonment shall terminate. A is sentenced to a fine of one thousand rupees and to four months’ imprisonment in default of payment. Here, if seven hundred and fifty rupees of the fine be paid or levied before the expiration of one month of the imprisonment, A will be discharged as soon as the first month has expired. If seven hundred and fifty rupees be paid or levied at the time of the expiration of the first month, or at any later time while A continues in imprisonment, A will be immediately discharged. If five hundred rupees of the fine be paid or levied before the expiration of two months of the imprisonment, A will be discharged as soon as the two months are completed. If five hundred rupees be paid or levied at the time of the expiration of those two months, or at any later time while A continues in imprisonment, A will be immediately discharged. The fine, or any part thereof which remains unpaid, may be levied at any time within six years after the passing of the sentence, and if, under the sentence, the offender be liable to imprisonment for a longer period than six years, then at any time previous to the expiration of that period; and the death of the offender does not discharge from the liability any property which would, after his death, be legally liable for his debts.

qas.question: What happens when no sum is expressed to which a fine may extend?

answers.text: The amount of fine to which the offender is liable is unlimited, but shall not be excessive.

qas.question: In what cases can the court impose imprisonment in default of fine?

answers.text: In every case of an offence punishable with imprisonment as well as fine, or punishable with imprisonment or fine, or with fine only.

qas.question: What is the maximum term of imprisonment in default of payment of fine for offences punishable with imprisonment as well as fine?

answers.text: The term shall not exceed one-fourth of the term of imprisonment which is the maximum fixed for the offence.

qas.question: What type of imprisonment is imposed if the offence is punishable with fine or community service?

answers.text: The imprisonment shall be simple.

qas.question: What is the maximum term of imprisonment for a fine not exceeding five thousand rupees?

answers.text: Two months.

qas.question: What is the maximum term of imprisonment for a fine exceeding ten thousand rupees?

answers.text: One year.

qas.question: When does imprisonment imposed in default of fine terminate?

answers.text: Whenever that fine is either paid or levied by process of law.

qas.question: How is the term of imprisonment reduced if a proportion of the fine is paid before the expiration of the term?

answers.text: The imprisonment shall terminate if the term suffered is proportional to the unpaid part of the fine.

qas.question: What is the total term of imprisonment imposed on A for non-payment of a fine of one thousand rupees?

answers.text: Four months.

qas.question: How long can the fine be levied after the passing of the sentence?

answers.text: Any time within six years after the passing of the sentence.

qas.question: Does the death of the offender discharge the fine liability?

answers.text: No, the death of the offender does not discharge the liability.

qas.question: What happens to the offender's property after death regarding unpaid fines?

answers.text: It remains legally liable for the offender's debts.

paragraphs.context: A consent is not such a consent as is intended by any section of this Sanhita–– (a) if the consent is given by a person under fear of injury, or under a misconception of fact, and if the person doing the act knows, or has reason to believe, that the consent was given in consequence of such fear or misconception; or (b) if the consent is given by a person who, from unsoundness of mind, or intoxication, is unable to understand the nature and consequence of that to which he gives his consent; or (c) unless the contrary appears from the context, if the consent is given by a person who is under twelve years of age.

qas.question: When is consent not valid according to the Sanhita?

answers.text: If the consent is given by a person under fear of injury, or under a misconception of fact; if it is given by a person of unsound mind or intoxicated; or if the person is under twelve years of age.

qas.question: What happens if consent is given under fear of injury or misconception of fact?

answers.text: The consent is not valid if the person doing the act knows, or has reason to believe, that the consent was given in consequence of such fear or misconception.

qas.question: Is consent valid if given by a person of unsound mind or intoxication?

answers.text: No, if the person is unable to understand the nature and consequence of that to which he gives his consent.

qas.question: Is consent valid if given by a person under twelve years of age?

answers.text: No, unless the contrary appears from the context.

paragraphs.context: The exceptions in sections 25, 26 and 27 do not extend to acts which are offences independently of any harm which they may cause, or be intended to cause, or be known to be likely to cause, to the person giving the consent, or on whose behalf the consent is given.

qas.question: Do the exceptions in sections 25, 26, and 27 apply to acts that are offences independently of harm caused?

answers.text: No, they do not extend to acts which are offences independently of any harm caused.

paragraphs.context: Causing miscarriage (unless caused in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of the woman) is an offence independently of any harm which it may cause or be intended to cause to the woman. Therefore, it is not an offence “by reason of such harm”; and the consent of the woman or of her guardian to the causing of such miscarriage does not justify the act.

qas.question: Is causing miscarriage an offence even with the woman's consent?

answers.text: Yes, causing miscarriage is an offence independently of any harm, unless caused in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of the woman.

paragraphs.context: Nothing is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause to a person for whose benefit it is done in good faith, even without that person’s consent, if the circumstances are such that it is impossible for that person to signify consent, or if that person is incapable of giving consent, and has no guardian or other person in lawful charge of him from whom it is possible to obtain consent in time for the thing to be done with benefit.

qas.question: When is an act not an offence even without the person’s consent?

answers.text: If the act is done in good faith for the person’s benefit, and it is impossible for that person to signify consent or obtain consent from a guardian in time.

paragraphs.context: Z is thrown from his horse, and is insensible. A, a surgeon, finds that Z requires to be trepanned. A, not intending Z’s death, but in good faith, for Z’s benefit, performs the trepan before Z recovers his power of judging for himself. A has committed no offence.

qas.question: Why is A not guilty of an offence for performing surgery on Z without consent?

answers.text: Because A acted in good faith for Z’s benefit before Z recovered his power of judging for himself.

paragraphs.context: Z is carried off by a tiger. A fires at the tiger knowing it to be likely that the shot may kill Z, but not intending to kill Z, and in good faith intending Z’s benefit. A’s bullet gives Z a mortal wound. A has committed no offence.

qas.question: Why is A not guilty of an offence for accidentally killing Z while firing at a tiger?

answers.text: Because A fired at the tiger in good faith intending Z’s benefit, and did not intend to kill Z.

paragraphs.context: A, a surgeon, sees a child suffer an accident which is likely to prove fatal unless an operation be immediately performed. There is no time to apply to the child’s guardian. A performs the operation in spite of the entreaties of the child, intending, in good faith, the child’s benefit. A has committed no offence.

qas.question: Why is A not guilty of an offence for performing an operation on a child without guardian consent?

answers.text: Because A acted in good faith intending the child’s benefit, and there was no time to obtain guardian consent.

paragraphs.context: A is in a house which is on fire, with Z, a child. People below hold out a blanket. A drops the child from the house top, knowing it to be likely that the fall may kill the child, but not intending to kill the child, and intending, in good faith, the child’s benefit. Here, even if the child is killed by the fall, A has committed no offence.

qas.question: Why is A not guilty of an offence for dropping Z from a burning house?

answers.text: Because A acted in good faith intending the child’s benefit and did not intend to kill the child.

paragraphs.context: Mere pecuniary benefit is not benefit within the meaning of sections 26, 27 and this section.

qas.question: Is pecuniary benefit considered a benefit under sections 26, 27, and 30?

answers.text: No, mere pecuniary benefit is not considered a benefit.

paragraphs.context: No communication made in good faith is an offence by reason of any harm to the person to whom it is made, if it is made for the benefit of that person.

qas.question: When is a communication made in good faith not considered an offence?

answers.text: If it is made for the benefit of that person.

paragraphs.context: A, a surgeon, in good faith, communicates to a patient his opinion that he cannot live. The patient dies in consequence of the shock. A has committed no offence, though he knew it to be likely that the communication might cause the patient’s death.

qas.question: Why is a surgeon not guilty of an offence for communicating a terminal opinion to a patient?

answers.text: Because the communication was made in good faith for the patient’s benefit.

paragraphs.context: Except murder, and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is compelled to do it by threats, which, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence.

qas.question: When is an act committed under threats not considered an offence?

answers.text: If the threats reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence.

qas.question: Which offences are excluded from the exception for acts compelled by threats?

answers.text: Murder, and offences against the State punishable with death.

paragraphs.context: A person who, of his own accord, or by reason of a threat of being beaten, joins a gang of dacoits, knowing their character, is not entitled to the benefit of this exception, on the ground of his having been compelled by his associates to do anything that is an offence by law.

qas.question: Can a person who joins a gang of dacoits under threat of being beaten claim the exception?

answers.text: No, he is not entitled to the benefit of this exception.

paragraphs.context: A person seized by a gang of dacoits, and forced, by threat of instant death, to do a thing which is an offence by law; for example, a smith compelled to take his tools and to force the door of a house for the dacoits to enter and plunder it, is entitled to the benefit of this exception.

qas.question: Can a person forced by a gang of dacoits under threat of instant death claim the exception?

answers.text: Yes, he is entitled to the benefit of this exception.

paragraphs.context: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or that it is intended to cause, or that it is known to be likely to cause, any harm, if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

qas.question: When is harm caused by an act not considered an offence?

answers.text: If the harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of such harm.

paragraphs.context: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

qas.question: When is an act done in the exercise of private defence not considered an offence?

answers.text: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

paragraphs.context: Every person has a right, subject to the restrictions contained in section 37, to defend— (a) his own body, and the body of any other person, against any offence affecting the human body; (b) the property, whether movable or immovable, of himself or of any other person, against any act which is an offence falling under the definition of theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass, or which is an attempt to commit theft, robbery, mischief or criminal trespass.

qas.question: What does the right of private defence include?

answers.text: The right to defend his own body and the body of any other person, and the property of himself or any other person against offences like theft, robbery, mischief, or criminal trespass.

paragraphs.context: When an act, which would otherwise be a certain offence, is not that offence, by reason of the youth, the want of maturity of understanding, the unsoundness of mind or the intoxication of the person doing that act, or by reason of any misconception on the part of that person, every person has the same right of private defence against that act which he would have if the act were that offence.

qas.question: Does the right of private defence apply against acts committed due to unsoundness of mind or intoxication?

answers.text: Yes, every person has the same right of private defence against that act as if the act were that offence.

paragraphs.context: Z, a person of unsound mind, attempts to kill A; Z is guilty of no offence. But A has the same right of private defence which he would have if Z were sane.

qas.question: Does A have the right of private defence against Z, a person of unsound mind, attempting to kill him?

answers.text: Yes, A has the same right of private defence which he would have if Z were sane.

paragraphs.context: A enters by night a house which he is legally entitled to enter. Z, in good faith, taking A for a house-breaker, attacks A. Here Z, by attacking A under this misconception, commits no offence. But A has the same right of private defence against Z, which he would have if Z were not acting under that misconception.

qas.question: Does A have the right of private defence against Z, who mistakenly attacks him thinking he is a house-breaker?

answers.text: Yes, A has the same right of private defence against Z, which he would have if Z were not acting under that misconception.

paragraphs.context: There is no right of private defence,–– (a) against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or of grievous hurt, if done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office, though that act, may not be strictly justifiable by law; (b) against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or of grievous hurt, if done, or attempted to be done, by the direction of a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office, though that direction may not be strictly justifiable by law; (c) in cases in which there is time to have recourse to the protection of the public authorities.

qas.question: When is there no right of private defence against acts of public servants?

answers.text: Against an act which does not reasonably cause the apprehension of death or of grievous hurt, if done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant acting in good faith under colour of his office.

qas.question: Is there a right of private defence if there is time to have recourse to public authorities?

answers.text: No, there is no right of private defence in cases in which there is time to have recourse to the protection of the public authorities.

paragraphs.context: The right of private defence in no case extends to the inflicting of more harm than it is necessary to inflict for the purpose of defence.

qas.question: Does the right of private defence allow for excessive harm?

answers.text: No, the right of private defence in no case extends to the inflicting of more harm than it is necessary to inflict for the purpose of defence.

paragraphs.context: A person is not deprived of the right of private defence against an act done, or attempted to be done, by a public servant, as such, unless he knows or has reason to believe, that the person doing the act is such public servant.

qas.question: When is a person deprived of the right of private defence against a public servant?

answers.text: If he knows or has reason to believe, that the person doing the act is such public servant.

paragraphs.context: The right of private defence of the body extends, under the restrictions specified in section 37, to the voluntary causing of death or of any other harm to the assailant, if the offence which occasions the exercise of the right be of any of the descriptions hereinafter enumerated, namely: (a) such an assault as may reasonably cause the apprehension that death will otherwise be the consequence of such assault; (b) such an assault as may reasonably cause the apprehension that grievous hurt will otherwise be the consequence of such assault; (c) an assault with the intention of committing rape; (d) an assault with the intention of gratifying unnatural lust; (e) an assault with the intention of kidnapping or abducting; (f) an assault with the intention of wrongfully confining a person, under circumstances which may reasonably cause him to apprehend that he will be unable to have recourse to the public authorities for his release; (g) an act of throwing or administering acid or an attempt to throw or administer acid which may reasonably cause the apprehension that grievous hurt will otherwise be the consequence of such act.

qas.question: When does the right of private defence of the body extend to causing death?

answers.text: If the offence which occasions the exercise of the right includes assaults that cause apprehension of death, grievous hurt, or involve rape, kidnapping, wrongful confinement, or acts like administering acid.

qas.question: Does the right of private defence extend to an assault with the intention of committing rape?

answers.text: Yes, the right of private defence extends to an assault with the intention of committing rape.

paragraphs.context: The right of private defence of the body commences as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger to the body arises from an attempt or threat to commit the offence though the offence may not have been committed; and it continues as long as such apprehension of danger to the body continues.

qas.question: When does the right of private defence of the body commence and continue?

answers.text: It commences as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger to the body arises from an attempt or threat to commit the offence and continues as long as such apprehension continues.

paragraphs.context: The right of private defence of property extends, under the restrictions specified in section 37, to the voluntary causing of death or of any other harm to the wrong-doer, if the offence, the committing of which, or the attempting to commit which, occasions the exercise of the right, be an offence of any of the descriptions hereinafter enumerated, namely: (a) robbery; (b) house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise; (c) mischief by fire or any explosive substance committed on any building, tent or vessel, which building, tent or vessel is used as a human dwelling, or as a place for the custody of property; (d) theft, mischief, or house-trespass, under such circumstances as may reasonably cause apprehension that death or grievous hurt will be the consequence, if such right of private defence is not exercised.

qas.question: When does the right of private defence of property extend to causing death?

answers.text: If the offence occasions the exercise of the right and involves robbery, house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, mischief by fire, or theft under circumstances causing apprehension of death or grievous hurt.

paragraphs.context: If in the exercise of the right of private defence against an assault which reasonably causes the apprehension of death, the defender be so situated that he cannot effectually exercise that right without risk of harm to an innocent person, his right of private defence extends to the running of that risk.

qas.question: Does the right of private defence extend to risking harm to an innocent person?

answers.text: Yes, if the defender cannot effectually exercise that right without risking harm to an innocent person.

paragraphs.context: A is attacked by a mob who attempt to murder him. He cannot effectually exercise his right of private defence without firing on the mob, and he cannot fire without risk of harming young children who are mingled with the mob. A commits no offence if by so firing he harms any of the children.

qas.question: Why does A not commit an offence for harming children while defending himself against a mob?

answers.text: Because A could not effectually exercise his right of private defence without risking harm to the children.

paragraphs.context: A person abets the doing of a thing, who— (a) instigates any person to do that thing; (b) engages with one or more other person or persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy, and in order to the doing of that thing; (c) intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

qas.question: Who is considered to abet the doing of a thing?

answers.text: A person who instigates any person to do that thing; engages in conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place; or intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.

paragraphs.context: A person who, by wilful misrepresentation, or by wilful concealment of a material fact which he is bound to disclose, voluntarily causes or procures, or attempts to cause or procure, a thing to be done, is said to instigate the doing of that thing.

qas.question: What constitutes instigating the doing of a thing?

answers.text: Wilful misrepresentation or wilful concealment of a material fact which he is bound to disclose, and voluntarily causing, procuring, or attempting to procure a thing to be done.

paragraphs.context: A, a public officer, is authorised by a warrant from a Court to apprehend Z. B, knowing that fact and also that C is not Z, wilfully represents to A that C is Z, and thereby intentionally causes A to apprehend C. Here B abets by instigation the apprehension of C.

qas.question: How does B abet the apprehension of C?

answers.text: By wilfully representing to A that C is Z, thereby intentionally causing A to apprehend C.

paragraphs.context: Whoever, either prior to or at the time of the commission of an act, does anything in order to facilitate the commission of that act, and thereby facilitates the commission thereof, is said to aid the doing of that act.

qas.question: When is a person said to aid the doing of an act?

answers.text: When they do anything, either prior to or at the time of the commission of an act, to facilitate the commission of that act.

paragraphs.context: To constitute the offence of abetment it is not necessary that the act abetted should be committed, or that the effect requisite to constitute the offence should be caused.

qas.question: Is it necessary for the act abetted to be committed to constitute the offence of abetment?

answers.text: No, it is not necessary that the act abetted should be committed.

paragraphs.context: A instigates B to murder C. B refuses to do so. A is guilty of abetting B to commit murder.

qas.question: Is A guilty of abetment if B refuses to murder C after being instigated?

answers.text: Yes, A is guilty of abetting B to commit murder.

paragraphs.context: A, with a guilty intention, abets a child or a person of unsound mind to commit an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence, and having the same intention as A. Here A, whether the act be committed or not, is guilty of abetting an offence.

qas.question: Is A guilty of abetment if he instigates a child or a person of unsound mind to commit an offence?

answers.text: Yes, A is guilty of abetting an offence, whether the act is committed or not.

paragraphs.context: A instigates B to set fire to a dwelling-house. B, in consequence of his unsoundness of mind, being incapable of knowing the nature of the act, sets fire to the house in consequence of A’s instigation. B has committed no offence, but A is guilty of abetting the offence of setting fire to a dwelling-house, and is liable to the punishment provided for that offence.

qas.question: Is A guilty of abetment if B, an unsound person, sets fire to a house due to A's instigation?

answers.text: Yes, A is guilty of abetting the offence of setting fire to a dwelling-house.

paragraphs.context: The abetment of an offence being an offence, the abetment of such an abetment is also an offence.

qas.question: Is the abetment of an abetment considered an offence?

answers.text: Yes, the abetment of an abetment is also an offence.

paragraphs.context: A man is said to commit 'rape' if he— (a) penetrates his penis, to any extent, into the vagina, mouth, urethra, or anus of a woman or makes her do so with him or any other person; or (b) inserts, to any extent, any object or a part of the body, not being the penis, into the vagina, the urethra, or anus of a woman or makes her do so with him or any other person; or (c) manipulates any part of the body of a woman so as to cause penetration into the vagina, urethra, anus, or any part of the body of such woman or makes her do so with him or any other person; or (d) applies his mouth to the vagina, anus, or urethra of a woman or makes her do so with him or any other person, under the circumstances falling under any of the following seven descriptions: (i) against her will; (ii) without her consent; (iii) with her consent, when her consent has been obtained by putting her or any person in whom she is interested, in fear of death or hurt; (iv) with her consent, when the man knows that he is not her husband and that her consent is given because she believes that he is another man to whom she is or believes herself to be lawfully married; (v) with her consent when, at the time of giving such consent, by reason of unsoundness of mind or intoxication or the administration by him personally or through another of any stupefying or unwholesome substance, she is unable to understand the nature and consequences of that to which she gives consent; (vi) with or without her consent, when she is under eighteen years of age; (vii) when she is unable to communicate consent.

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than one year.

paragraphs.context: Any man who commits the offence specified in clause (i) or clause (ii) or clause (iii) of sub-section (1) shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for committing offences under clauses (i), (ii), or (iii) of sub-section (1)?

answers.text: Rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

paragraphs.context: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman or abets such act with the intention of disrobing or compelling her to be naked, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than three years but which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for assaulting a woman with the intent to disrobe?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than three years but which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever watches, or captures the image of a woman engaging in a private act in circumstances where she would usually have the expectation of not being observed either by the perpetrator or by any other person at the behest of the perpetrator or disseminates such image shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than one year, but which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for a first conviction under voyeurism?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than one year, but which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Where the victim consents to the capture of the images or any act, but not to their dissemination to third persons and where such image or act is disseminated, such dissemination shall be considered an offence under this section.

qas.question: What is considered an offence regarding dissemination of images under this section?

answers.text: Dissemination of images or acts without the victim's consent to third persons.

paragraphs.context: Any man who follows a woman and contacts, or attempts to contact such woman to foster personal interaction repeatedly despite a clear indication of disinterest by such woman; or monitors the use by a woman of the internet, e-mail or any other form of electronic communication, commits the offence of stalking.

qas.question: What actions constitute the offence of stalking?

answers.text: Following a woman and contacting or attempting to contact her despite a clear indication of disinterest, or monitoring her use of the internet, e-mail or any other form of electronic communication.

paragraphs.context: Whoever commits the offence of stalking shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and be punished on a second or subsequent conviction, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for stalking on the first conviction?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever, intending to insult the modesty of any woman, utters any words, makes any sound or gesture, or exhibits any object in any form, intending that such word or sound shall be heard, or that such gesture or object shall be seen, by such woman, or intrudes upon the privacy of such woman, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, and also with fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for intending to insult the modesty of a woman?

answers.text: Simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, and also with fine.

paragraphs.context: Where the death of a woman is caused by any burns or bodily injury or occurs otherwise than under normal circumstances within seven years of her marriage and it is shown that soon before her death she was subjected to cruelty or harassment by her husband or any relative of her husband for, or in connection with, any demand for dowry, such death shall be called 'dowry death', and such husband or relative shall be deemed to have caused her death.

qas.question: What is the definition of 'dowry death'?

answers.text: The death of a woman caused by burns or bodily injury, or occurring otherwise than under normal circumstances within seven years of her marriage, and it is shown that she was subjected to cruelty or harassment for dowry by her husband or his relatives.

paragraphs.context: Whoever commits dowry death shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than seven years but which may extend to imprisonment for life.

qas.question: What is the punishment for committing dowry death?

answers.text: Imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than seven years but which may extend to imprisonment for life.

paragraphs.context: Every man who by deceit causes any woman who is not lawfully married to him to believe that she is lawfully married to him and to cohabit or have sexual intercourse with him in that belief, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for deceitfully inducing a woman to believe she is lawfully married?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever, having a husband or wife living, marries in any case in which such marriage is void by reason of its taking place during the life of such husband or wife, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for marrying again during the lifetime of a spouse?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever commits the offence under section 88 without the consent of the woman, whether the woman is quick with child or not, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for causing a miscarriage without the woman's consent?

answers.text: Imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever, with intent to cause the miscarriage of a woman with child, does any act which causes the death of such woman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. Where the act is done without the consent of the woman, shall be punishable either with imprisonment for life, or with the punishment specified in said sub-section.

qas.question: What is the punishment for causing the death of a woman while intending to cause a miscarriage?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment if the act is done without the consent of the woman?

answers.text: Imprisonable either with imprisonment for life, or with the punishment specified in said sub-section.

paragraphs.context: Whoever before the birth of any child does any act with the intention of thereby preventing that child from being born alive or causing it to die after its birth, and does by such act prevent that child from being born alive, or causes it to die after its birth, shall, if such act be not caused in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of the mother, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, or with fine, or with both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for preventing a child from being born alive or causing it to die after birth?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, or with fine, or with both.

paragraphs.context: Whoever does any act under such circumstances, that if he thereby caused death he would be guilty of culpable homicide, and does by such act cause the death of a quick unborn child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for causing the death of a quick unborn child by an act amounting to culpable homicide?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever being the father or mother of a child under the age of twelve years, or having the care of such child, shall expose or leave such child in any place with the intention of wholly abandoning such child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for abandoning a child under the age of twelve years?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.

paragraphs.context: Whoever, by secretly burying or otherwise disposing of the dead body of a child whether such child die before or after or during its birth, intentionally conceals or endeavours to conceal the birth of such child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for concealing the birth of a child by secretly disposing of the body?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

paragraphs.context: Whoever hires, employs or engages any child to commit an offence shall be punished with imprisonment of either description which shall not be less than three years but which may extend to ten years and with fine; and if the offence be committed shall also be punished with the punishment provided for that offence as if the offence has been committed by such person himself.

qas.question: What is the punishment for hiring or employing a child to commit an offence?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description which shall not be less than three years but which may extend to ten years and with fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever, by any means whatsoever, induces any child to go from any place or to do any act with intent that such child may be, or knowing that it is likely that such child will be, forced or seduced to illicit intercourse with another person shall be punishable with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for inducing a child with intent to force or seduce them to illicit intercourse?

answers.text: Imprisonment which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

paragraphs.context: Whoever kidnaps or abducts any child under the age of ten years with the intention of taking dishonestly any movable property from the person of such child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for kidnapping a child under ten years with intent to steal from them?

answers.text: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.

data.title: Indian Penal Code - Explanation on Homicide and Murder

paragraphs.context: Explanation 2.—Where death is caused by bodily injury, the person who causes such bodily injury shall be deemed to have caused the death, although by resorting to proper remedies and skilful treatment the death might have been prevented.

qas.question: Who is deemed to have caused death in the case of bodily injury?

answers.text: the person who causes such bodily injury

qas.question: Can death by bodily injury still be considered caused if it could have been prevented by treatment?

answers.text: Yes

paragraphs.context: Explanation 3.—The causing of the death of a child in the mother’s womb is not homicide. But it may amount to culpable homicide to cause the death of a living child, if any part of that child has been brought forth, though the child may not have breathed or been completely born.

qas.question: Is causing the death of a child in the mother’s womb considered homicide?

answers.text: No

qas.question: Under what condition can the death of a living child be considered culpable homicide?

answers.text: if any part of that child has been brought forth

paragraphs.context: 101. Except in the cases hereinafter excepted, culpable homicide is murder,–– (a) if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death; or (b) if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death of the person to whom the harm is caused; or (c) if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing bodily injury to any person and the bodily injury intended to be inflicted is sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death; or (d) if the person committing the act by which the death is caused, knows that it is so imminently dangerous that it must, in all probability, cause death, or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, and commits such act without any excuse for incurring the risk of causing death or such injury as aforesaid.

qas.question: What are the conditions under which culpable homicide is considered murder?

answers.text: (a) if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death; or (b) if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as the offender knows to be likely to cause the death of the person to whom the harm is caused; or (c) if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing bodily injury to any person and the bodily injury intended to be inflicted is sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death; or (d) if the person committing the act by which the death is caused, knows that it is so imminently dangerous that it must, in all probability, cause death, or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, and commits such act without any excuse for incurring the risk of causing death or such injury as aforesaid.

qas.question: What must a person know about their act for it to be considered murder under condition (d)?

answers.text: that it is so imminently dangerous that it must, in all probability, cause death, or such bodily injury as is likely to cause death

paragraphs.context: Exception 1.—Culpable homicide is not murder if the offender, whilst deprived of the power of self-control by grave and sudden provocation, causes the death of the person who gave the provocation or causes the death of any other person by mistake or accident: Provided that the provocation is not,–– (a) sought or voluntarily provoked by the offender as an excuse for killing or doing harm to any person; (b) given by anything done in obedience to the law, or by a public servant in the lawful exercise of the powers of such public servant; (c) given by anything done in the lawful exercise of the right of private defence.

qas.question: What is the exception for culpable homicide not being considered murder?

answers.text: if the offender, whilst deprived of the power of self-control by grave and sudden provocation, causes the death of the person who gave the provocation or causes the death of any other person by mistake or accident

qas.question: What conditions prevent provocation from being considered valid for this exception?

answers.text: (a) sought or voluntarily provoked by the offender as an excuse for killing or doing harm to any person; (b) given by anything done in obedience to the law, or by a public servant in the lawful exercise of the powers of such public servant; (c) given by anything done in the lawful exercise of the right of private defence.

data.title: Indian Penal Code - Illustrations and Exceptions

paragraphs.context: Illustrations. (a) A, under the influence of passion excited by a provocation given by Z, intentionally kills Y, Z’s child. This is murder, in as much as the provocation was not given by the child, and the death of the child was not caused by accident or misfortune in doing an act caused by the provocation.

qas.question: Why is A's act considered murder in Illustration (a)?

answers.text: the provocation was not given by the child, and the death of the child was not caused by accident or misfortune in doing an act caused by the provocation

paragraphs.context: (b) Y gives grave and sudden provocation to A. A, on this provocation, fires a pistol at Y, neither intending nor knowing himself to be likely to kill Z, who is near him, but out of sight. A kills Z. Here A has not committed murder, but merely culpable homicide.

qas.question: What offense does A commit in Illustration (b)?

answers.text: culpable homicide

paragraphs.context: (c) A is lawfully arrested by Z, a bailiff. A is excited to sudden and violent passion by the arrest, and kills Z. This is murder, in as much as the provocation was given by a thing done by a public servant in the exercise of his powers.

qas.question: Why is A's act in Illustration (c) considered murder?

answers.text: the provocation was given by a thing done by a public servant in the exercise of his powers

paragraphs.context: (d) A appears as a witness before Z, a Magistrate. Z says that he does not believe a word of A’s deposition, and that A has perjured himself. A is moved to sudden passion by these words, and kills Z. This is murder.

qas.question: What provoked A to commit murder in Illustration (d)?

answers.text: Z says that he does not believe a word of A’s deposition, and that A has perjured himself

paragraphs.context: (e) A attempts to pull Z’s nose. Z, in the exercise of the right of private defence, lays hold of A to prevent him from doing so. A is moved to sudden and violent passion in consequence, and kills Z. This is murder, in as much as the provocation was giving by a thing done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

qas.question: Why is A's act in Illustration (e) considered murder?

answers.text: the provocation was giving by a thing done in the exercise of the right of private defence

paragraphs.context: (f) Z strikes B. B is by this provocation excited to violent rage. A, a bystander, intending to take advantage of B’s rage, and to cause him to kill Z, puts a knife into B’s hand for that purpose. B kills Z. Here B may have committed only culpable homicide, but A is guilty of murder.

qas.question: Why is A guilty of murder in Illustration (f)?

answers.text: A, a bystander, intending to take advantage of B’s rage, and to cause him to kill Z, puts a knife into B’s hand for that purpose

paragraphs.context: Exception 2.—Culpable homicide is not murder if the offender in the exercise in good faith of the right of private defence of person or property, exceeds the power given to him by law and causes the death of the person against whom he is exercising such right of defence without premeditation, and without any intention of doing more harm than is necessary for the purpose of such defence.

qas.question: Under what condition does Exception 2 apply to culpable homicide?

answers.text: if the offender in the exercise in good faith of the right of private defence of person or property, exceeds the power given to him by law and causes the death of the person against whom he is exercising such right of defence without premeditation, and without any intention of doing more harm than is necessary for the purpose of such defence

paragraphs.context: Exception 3.—Culpable homicide is not murder if the offender, being a public servant or aiding a public servant acting for the advancement of public justice, exceeds the powers given to him by law, and causes death by doing an act which he, in good faith, believes to be lawful and necessary for the due discharge of his duty as such public servant and without ill-will towards the person whose death is caused.

qas.question: When does Exception 3 apply to culpable homicide?

answers.text: if the offender, being a public servant or aiding a public servant acting for the advancement of public justice, exceeds the powers given to him by law, and causes death by doing an act which he, in good faith, believes to be lawful and necessary for the due discharge of his duty as such public servant and without ill-will towards the person whose death is caused

paragraphs.context: Exception 4.—Culpable homicide is not murder if it is committed without premeditation in a sudden fight in the heat of passion upon a sudden quarrel and without the offender’s having taken undue advantage or acted in a cruel or unusual manner. Explanation.—It is immaterial in such cases which party offers the provocation or commits the first assault.

qas.question: What conditions must be met for Exception 4 to apply to culpable homicide?

answers.text: if it is committed without premeditation in a sudden fight in the heat of passion upon a sudden quarrel and without the offender’s having taken undue advantage or acted in a cruel or unusual manner

paragraphs.context: Exception 5.—Culpable homicide is not murder when the person whose death is caused, being above the age of eighteen years, suffers death or takes the risk of death with his own consent.

qas.question: When does Exception 5 apply to culpable homicide?

answers.text: when the person whose death is caused, being above the age of eighteen years, suffers death or takes the risk of death with his own consent

paragraphs.context: Illustration. A, by instigation, voluntarily causes Z, a child to commit suicide. Here, on account of Z’s youth, he was incapable of giving consent to his own death; A has therefore abetted murder.

qas.question: Why is A guilty of abetting murder in the Exception 5 illustration?

answers.text: on account of Z’s youth, he was incapable of giving consent to his own death

data.title: Indian Penal Code - Culpable Homicide, Murder, and Related Offenses

paragraphs.context: Culpable homicide by causing death of person other than person whose death was intended. 102. If a person, by doing anything which he intends or knows to be likely to cause death, commits culpable homicide by causing the death of any person, whose death he neither intends nor knows himself to be likely to cause, the culpable homicide committed by the offender is of the description of which it would have been if he had caused the death of the person whose death he intended or knew himself to be likely to cause.

qas.question: What happens if a person causes the death of someone other than the person they intended to kill, according to Section 102?

answers.text: the culpable homicide committed by the offender is of the description of which it would have been if he had caused the death of the person whose death he intended or knew himself to be likely to cause

paragraphs.context: Punishment for murder. 103. (1) Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine. (2) When a group of five or more persons acting in concert commits murder on the ground of race, caste or community, sex, place of birth, language, personal belief or any other similar ground each member of such group shall be punished with death or with imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for committing murder according to Section 103(1)?

answers.text: punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine

qas.question: What happens when a group of five or more persons commits murder on specific grounds under Section 103(2)?

answers.text: each member of such group shall be punished with death or with imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine

paragraphs.context: Punishment for murder by life-convict. 104. Whoever, being under sentence of imprisonment for life, commits murder, shall be punished with death or with imprisonment for life, which shall mean the remainder of that person’s natural life.

qas.question: What happens if a life convict commits murder according to Section 104?

answers.text: punished with death or with imprisonment for life, which shall mean the remainder of that person’s natural life

paragraphs.context: Punishment for culpable homicide not amounting to murder. 105. Whoever commits culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than five years but which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine, if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death, or of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death; or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years and with fine, if the act is done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause death, but without any intention to cause death, or to cause such bodily injury as is likely to cause death.

qas.question: What is the punishment for culpable homicide not amounting to murder according to Section 105?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than five years but which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine

paragraphs.context: Causing death by negligence. 106. (1) Whoever causes death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if such act is done by a registered medical practitioner while performing medical procedure, he shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation.— For the purposes of this sub-section, “registered medical practitioner” means a medical practitioner who possesses any medical qualification recognised under the National Medical Commission Act, 2019 and whose name has been entered in the National Medical Register or a State Medical Register under that Act.

qas.question: What is the punishment for causing death by negligence according to Section 106(1)?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine

qas.question: What is the punishment for a registered medical practitioner causing death by negligence while performing a medical procedure according to Section 106(1)?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine

paragraphs.context: (2) Whoever causes death of any person by rash and negligent driving of vehicle not amounting to culpable homicide, and escapes without reporting it to a police officer or a Magistrate soon after the incident, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description of a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What happens if a person causes death by rash and negligent driving and escapes without reporting the incident?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment of either description of a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine

paragraphs.context: Abetment of suicide of child or person of unsound mind. 107. If any child, any person of unsound mind, any delirious person or any person in a state of intoxication, commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide, shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for abetment of suicide of a child or person of unsound mind according to Section 107?

answers.text: punished with death or imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine

paragraphs.context: Abetment of suicide. 108. If any person commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qas.question: What is the punishment for abetment of suicide according to Section 108?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine

paragraphs.context: Attempt to murder. 109. (1) Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if hurt is caused to any person by such act, the offender shall be liable either to imprisonment for life, or to such punishment as is hereinbefore mentioned.

qas.question: What is the punishment for attempt to murder according to Section 109(1)?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine

paragraphs.context: (2) When any person offending under sub-section (1) is under sentence of imprisonment for life, he may, if hurt is caused, be punished with death or with imprisonment for life, which shall mean the remainder of that person’s natural life.

qas.question: What happens if a person, already under sentence of imprisonment for life, offends under sub-section (1) and causes hurt?

answers.text: he may, if hurt is caused, be punished with death or with imprisonment for life, which shall mean the remainder of that person’s natural life

paragraphs.context: Illustrations. (a) A shoots at Z with intention to kill him, under such circumstances that, if death ensued, A would be guilty of murder. A is liable to punishment under this section. (b) A, with the intention of causing the death of a child of tender years, exposes it in a desert place. A has committed the offence defined by this section, though the death of the child does not ensue. (c) A, intending to murder Z, buys a gun and loads it. A has not yet committed the offence. A fires the gun at Z. He has committed the offence defined in this section, and, if by such firing he wounds Z, he is liable to the punishment provided by the latter part of sub-section (1). (d) A, intending to murder Z by poison, purchases poison and mixes the same with food which remains in A’s keeping; A has not yet committed the offence defined in this section. A places the food on Z’s table or delivers it to Z’s servants to place it on Z’s table. A has committed the offence defined in this section.

qas.question: What happens in illustration (a) when A shoots at Z with the intention to kill him?

answers.text: A is liable to punishment under this section

qas.question: In illustration (b), what does A do with the intention of causing the death of a child?

answers.text: A exposes it in a desert place

qas.question: What happens when A, intending to murder Z, buys a gun and loads it in illustration (c)?

answers.text: A has not yet committed the offence

qas.question: What happens when A, intending to murder Z by poison, purchases poison and places it on Z’s table in illustration (d)?

answers.text: A has committed the offence defined in this section

paragraphs.context: 110. Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both; and, if hurt is caused to any person by such act, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.

qas.question: What is the punishment for committing an act with intention or knowledge that would cause culpable homicide not amounting to murder under Section 110?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both

qas.question: What happens if hurt is caused to any person by an act under Section 110?

answers.text: punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both

qas.question: What does A do in the illustration related to attempt to commit culpable homicide?

answers.text: fires a pistol at Z, under such circumstances that if he thereby caused death, he would be guilty of culpable homicide not amounting to murder

paragraphs.context: 111. (1) Any continuing unlawful activity including kidnapping, robbery, vehicle theft, extortion, land grabbing, contract killing, economic offence, cyber-crimes, trafficking of persons, drugs, weapons or illicit goods or services, human trafficking for prostitution or ransom, by any person or a group of persons acting in concert, singly or jointly, either as a member of an organised crime syndicate or on behalf of such syndicate, by use of violence, threat of violence, intimidation, coercion, or by any other unlawful means to obtain direct or indirect material benefit including a financial benefit, shall constitute organised crime. Explanation.—For the purposes of this sub-section,–– (i) “organised crime syndicate” means a group of two or more persons who, acting either singly or jointly, as a syndicate or gang indulge in any continuing unlawful activity; (ii) “continuing unlawful activity” means an activity prohibited by law which is a cognizable offence punishable with imprisonment of three years or more, undertaken by any person, either singly or jointly, as a member of an organised crime syndicate.

paragraphs.context: Explanation.—A person is not said voluntarily to cause grievous hurt except when he both causes grievous hurt and intends or knows himself to be likely to cause grievous hurt. But he is said voluntarily to cause grievous hurt, if intending or knowing himself to be likely to cause grievous hurt of one kind, he actually causes grievous hurt of another kind. Illustration. A, intending of knowing himself to be likely permanently to disfigure Z’s face, gives Z a blow which does not permanently disfigure Z’s face, but which causes Z to suffer severe bodily pain for the space of fifteen days. A has voluntarily caused grievous hurt.
